steps.title: Identify the grammatical structure around the blank
steps.content: The sentence contains two independent clauses: (1) "Saville is not the protagonist of Shelley's [novel]" and (2) "she's the 'occasion' of it." The word "rather" is a conjunctive adverb introducing the second clause. The rule: when a conjunctive adverb joins two independent clauses, use a semicolon before it and a comma after it.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'novel': Run-on sentence error — the two independent clauses are fused with no punctuation or conjunction between them. ✗
  • Choice B — 'novel,': Comma splice error — a comma alone cannot join two independent clauses without a coordinating conjunction. ✗
  • Choice C — 'novel; rather,': Correct — the semicolon separates the two independent clauses and the comma follows the conjunctive adverb "rather," conforming to Standard English. ✓
  • Choice D — 'novel, rather,': Comma splice error — the comma before "rather" cannot replace the required semicolon when joining two independent clauses. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
